topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The clinking of champagne glasses seemed to amplify the sharp edges of the evening. Sarah watched from across the crowded ballroom, a tight knot forming in her chest. Then, across the room, she saw him. Mark. He was laughing, a bright, unrestrained sound that scraped against her nerves. When their eyes met, the laughter died in his throat, replaced by a sudden, brittle stillness. He offered a quick, practiced smile, the kind reserved for acquaintances, and then turned back to the group. Sarah felt a flush creep up her neck, hot and undeniable. She deliberately turned her attention to a passing waiter, focusing intensely on the silver tray, trying to keep her hands steady. Every casual gesture he made—the way he leaned in to share a joke, the easy familiarity in his posture—felt like a deliberate dismissal of everything she thought they had. She took a slow, deliberate sip of her wine, the liquid burning a little on her tongue, and decided the best course of action was to become utterly, impeccably aloof.

[story 2]

"Oh, look who it is," Chloe murmured, nudging Maya as a mutual friend, Jessica, navigated the dance floor. Maya froze mid-step. There he was. David. He looked… polished. Too polished. When he spotted her, his smile faltered for a fraction of a second, a subtle tightening around his jaw that she caught immediately. It wasn't a warm greeting; it was an acknowledgment of a shared history that felt suddenly heavy and unwelcome. Maya felt a strange tightening in her throat, a sudden dryness that made swallowing difficult. She forced a bright, almost too-wide smile onto her face, the muscles in her cheeks feeling strained. She managed a small nod, a purely functional movement, and then pivoted sharply, walking towards the bar, needing the cool, impersonal surface of the counter beneath her fingertips to anchor her. She kept her gaze fixed on the condensation running down the glass, refusing to let her eyes linger on the familiar silhouette.

[story 3]

I had been trying to enjoy the symphony of the wedding—the soaring music, the happy chatter—but the atmosphere felt suddenly thin, stretched taut. Then, he appeared. Liam. He was standing near the entrance, talking animatedly with the groom, and when he glanced over, his expression shifted. It wasn't recognition; it was a brief, almost imperceptible flicker of something guarded, quickly masked by a casual wave. A cold sensation washed over me, starting in my stomach and spreading outward. I felt my shoulders tense up, a defensive hunch taking hold. I deliberately lowered my head, pretending to be deeply engrossed in examining a piece of artwork on the wall, acutely aware of the way my fingers gripped the frame. Every breath felt shallow, as if the sheer proximity of him was physically draining the air from my lungs. I focused on the texture of the canvas, willing myself to become invisible.
